title: W1 C Euclidean Spaces
body:
Mathematical Economics

Juan Carlos Carbajal

School of Economics UNSW–Sydney

Week 1C — Term 1, 2026

1/10  Euclidean Spaces

2/10

◦ The set of all natural numbers 1, 2, 3, 4, . . . is denoted N.

◦ The set of all integers . . . , −2, −1, 0, 1, 2, . . . is denoted Z.

◦ The set of all rational numbers is denoted Q:   p Q = : p, q ∈ Z, q ̸= 0 . q

3/10

◦ The set of all natural numbers 1, 2, 3, 4, . . . is denoted N.

◦ The set of all integers . . . , −2, −1, 0, 1, 2, . . . is denoted Z.

◦ The set of all rational numbers is denoted Q:   p Q = : p, q ∈ Z, q ̸= 0 . q

Observation N is countably infinite.

3/10

◦ The set of all natural numbers 1, 2, 3, 4, . . . is denoted N.

◦ The set of all integers . . . , −2, −1, 0, 1, 2, . . . is denoted Z.

◦ The set of all rational numbers is denoted Q:   p Q = : p, q ∈ Z, q ̸= 0 . q

Observation N is countably infinite.

Z and Q have the same cardinality as N —they are also countably infinite. ◦ There is a one-to-one association between N and each of these sets.

◦ Let’s look at Hilbert’s Hotel.

3/10

To show that Z is countably infinite: ◦ Set z = n2 ∈ Z if and only if n ∈ N is even.

◦ Set z = − n−1 ∈ Z if and only if n ∈ N is odd.

4/10

To show that Z is countably infinite: ◦ Set z = n2 ∈ Z if and only if n ∈ N is even.

◦ Set z = − n−1 ∈ Z if and only if n ∈ N is odd.

Showing that Q is countably infinite turns out to be equivalent to showing that the set of ordered pairs N × N is countably infinite.

(1, 1) (1, 2) (1, 3) (1, 4) ... (2, 1) (2, 2) (2, 3) (2, 4) ··· (3, 1) (3, 2) (3, 3) (3, 4) ··· (4, 1) (4, 2) (4, 3) (4, 4) ··· .. .. .. .. .. . . . . .

4/10

√

√

Proof p 2 ◦ Suppose there is a rational pq such that  q = 2.

◦ We can set p, q so that at least one of them is an odd number. Why? Assume p, q are both even. Now divide numerator and denominator by 2.

√

Proof p 2 ◦ Suppose there is a rational pq such that  q = 2.

◦ We can set p, q so that at least one of them is an odd number. Why? Assume p, q are both even. Now divide numerator and denominator by 2.

◦ Write the expression as p2 = 2q 2 — since p2 is even, so is p.

◦ Expressing p = 2k, we obtain p2 = 4k2 = 2q 2 .

√

Proof p 2 ◦ Suppose there is a rational pq such that  q = 2.

◦ We can set p, q so that at least one of them is an odd number. Why? Assume p, q are both even. Now divide numerator and denominator by 2.

◦ Write the expression as p2 = 2q 2 — since p2 is even, so is p.

◦ Expressing p = 2k, we obtain p2 = 4k2 = 2q 2 .

◦ Since q 2 = 2k2 is even, so is q.

√ 2 is a real number. The set of real numbers is denoted by R: R ≡ {x : − ∞ < x < + ∞}.

The construction of R hinges on the Completeness Axiom.

6/10

√ 2 is a real number. The set of real numbers is denoted by R: R ≡ {x : − ∞ < x < + ∞}.

The construction of R hinges on the Completeness Axiom.

R together with the standard arithmetic operations is an ordered space: for any x, y ∈ R, either x ≥ y or y ≥ x, where x ≥ y ⇐⇒ (x − y) ≥ 0.

6/10

√ 2 is a real number. The set of real numbers is denoted by R: R ≡ {x : − ∞ < x < + ∞}.

The construction of R hinges on the Completeness Axiom.

R together with the standard arithmetic operations is an ordered space: for any x, y ∈ R, either x ≥ y or y ≥ x, where x ≥ y ⇐⇒ (x − y) ≥ 0.

The n-dimensional Euclidean space is defined by: Rn = R × . . . × R ≡ x = (x1 , . . . , xn ) : xi ∈ R, i = 1, 2, . . . , n   | {z } n times

6/10   Euclidean Spaces

Write x ≥ y if xi ≥ yi for all i = 1, . . . , n. ◦ But note that Rn , unlike R, is not fully ordered.

◦ In R2 , x = (1, 0) and y = (0, 1) cannot be compared according to ≥.

7/10   Euclidean Spaces

Write x ≥ y if xi ≥ yi for all i = 1, . . . , n. ◦ But note that Rn , unlike R, is not fully ordered.

◦ In R2 , x = (1, 0) and y = (0, 1) cannot be compared according to ≥.

The Euclidean space Rn is a vector space with three basic operations. ◦ Vector addition: x + y = (x1 + y1 , . . . , xn + yn ).

◦ Scalar multiplication: α z = (αz1 , . . . , αzn ).

◦ Inner (dot) product: x · y = x1 y1 + . . . + xn yn .

These operations are very important.

7/10  Basic Operations in Euclidean Spaces

x2 x2 α>1 x+y

αz y z x x1 x1

x2 x2 α ∈ (0, 1) α<0

z z αz x1 x1 αz

8/10

◦ α(x + y) = α x + α y;

◦ (α + β)x = α x + β x;

◦ α(x + y) = α x + α y;

◦ (α + β)x = α x + β x;

◦ x · y = y · x;

◦ α(x · y) = (α x) · y = x · (α y);

◦ α(x + y) = α x + α y;

◦ (α + β)x = α x + β x;

◦ x · y = y · x;

◦ α(x · y) = (α x) · y = x · (α y);

◦ x · 0 = 0;

◦ x = x1 e1 + x2 e2 + . . . + xn en , where ei = (0, . . . , 0, 1, 0, . . . , 0).

Economic Applications

Example 1

There are n goods in a competitive market. ◦ Any good i = 1, . . . , n is consumed in non-negative quantities. ◦ All goods are assumed to be perfectly divisible.

10/10   Economic Applications

Example 1

There are n goods in a competitive market. ◦ Any good i = 1, . . . , n is consumed in non-negative quantities. ◦ All goods are assumed to be perfectly divisible.

The consumption space is X = Rn +.

◦ A consumption bundle x ∈ X is a vector x = (x1 , . . . , xn ) such that xi ≥ 0 for all i = 1, . . . , n.

10/10   Economic Applications

Example 1

There are n goods in a competitive market. ◦ Any good i = 1, . . . , n is consumed in non-negative quantities. ◦ All goods are assumed to be perfectly divisible.

The consumption space is X = Rn +.

◦ A consumption bundle x ∈ X is a vector x = (x1 , . . . , xn ) such that xi ≥ 0 for all i = 1, . . . , n.

Given prices p ≥ 0, the budget set for a consumer with wealth w is   B(p, w) = x ∈ X : p · x ≤ w .

10/10
